title: Python Data Wrangling for BI Developers
text: I started in Power BI — DAX, M, the whole ecosystem. Python came later, but it fundamentally changed what I could build. Here’s a practical introduction for BI developers making the same shift.

title: Python Data Wrangling for BI Developers
text:
Why Python alongside Power BI?
DAX is excellent for columnar calculations at query time. M is great for ETL within Power Query. But both have ceilings:

Statistical modeling beyond simple aggregates? Python.
NLP on text fields? Python.
API calls during data prep? Python (or a custom connector, but Python is faster to prototype).
Data quality scoring across millions of rows? Python.

The two ecosystems complement each other. I use Python for heavy lifting in data prep and modeling, then feed clean data into Power BI for visualization.

title: Python Data Wrangling for BI Developers
text:
The core libraries
import pandas as pd       # DataFrames — your M equivalent
import numpy as np        # Numerical operations
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  # Quick plots
If you’ve worked with Power Query tables, pandas DataFrames will feel familiar. Think of a DataFrame as a table you can manipulate with Python code.

title: Python Data Wrangling for BI Developers
text:
A real wrangling example
Here’s something I do regularly — standardizing vendor data from multiple sources that use inconsistent naming:
import pandas as pd
import re

df = pd.read_csv('vendor_data.csv')

# Standardize company names
def clean_company_name(name):
    if pd.isna(name):
        return 'Unknown'
    # Remove legal suffixes, strip whitespace, title case
    name = re.sub(r'\b(LLC|Inc|Corp|Ltd|Co)\.?\b', '', str(name))
    return ' '.join(name.strip().split()).title()

df['company_clean'] = df['company_raw'].apply(clean_company_name)

# Standardize date columns
df['event_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['event_date'], errors='coerce')
df['event_year'] = df['event_date'].dt.year
df['event_month'] = df['event_date'].dt.month

# Flag missing critical fields
df['has_missing'] = df[['company_clean', 'event_date', 'amount']].isna().any(axis=1)

print(df['has_missing'].value_counts())
The equivalent in M would be 3x longer and much harder to read.

title: Python Data Wrangling for BI Developers
section: Connecting Python output to Power BI
text:
Connecting Python output to Power BI
Once you have clean data in Python, there are a few ways to get it into Power BI:

Export to CSV/Parquet and load in Power Query
Python visuals (run Python directly in a Power BI report — limited but useful for custom charts)
Write to a database that Power BI connects to (preferred for production)

For production pipelines, I use option 3 — Python scripts run on a schedule (via Azure Functions or a VM), write results to SQL Server or Azure Data Lake, and Power BI picks them up on refresh.

title: Python Data Wrangling for BI Developers
text:
Where to go next

pandas documentation — it’s genuinely good: pandas.pydata.org
“Python for Data Analysis” by Wes McKinney — written by the creator of pandas
Kaggle’s free Python course — practical, short exercises

The learning curve from M to Python is real but shorter than you’d expect. Within a month of focused practice, you’ll find yourself reaching for Python first on complex data prep tasks.

title: Optimizing Power BI Report Load Times
text: Slow Power BI reports kill adoption. I’ve worked on dashboards that took 45 seconds to load and ones that loaded in under 2. Here’s what makes the difference.

title: Optimizing Power BI Report Load Times
text:
Diagnose before you optimize
Use Performance Analyzer (View → Performance Analyzer) to record exactly how long each visual takes to render and which DAX queries are running. Never guess — measure.
For deeper investigation, connect DAX Studio to your report and use Server Timings to see storage engine vs. formula engine time. If most of your time is in the formula engine, your DAX needs work. If it’s storage engine, your model structure needs work.

title: Optimizing Power BI Report Load Times
text:
DAX optimizations

Avoid iterator functions over large tables
SUMX, AVERAGEX, and friends iterate row by row. Over millions of rows, this is slow.
-- Slow
SlowMeasure = SUMX(Orders, Orders[Quantity] * Orders[UnitPrice])

-- Faster: pre-compute in a calculated column, or restructure
FastMeasure = SUMX(Orders, Orders[ExtendedPrice])
If the multiplication is always the same, make it a calculated column so it runs once at refresh time, not at query time.


Replace CALCULATE with simpler alternatives
Every CALCULATE modifies the filter context — it’s powerful but not free. For simple filtered aggregations, sometimes a relationship and a base measure is faster.


Use variables
Variables cache the result of an expression so it isn’t evaluated multiple times in the same DAX query:
SalesMeasure =
VAR TotalSales = SUM(Orders[Amount])
VAR Target = [SalesTarget]
RETURN
    DIVIDE(TotalSales - Target, Target)
Without VAR, SUM(Orders[Amount]) might be evaluated multiple times.

title: Optimizing Power BI Report Load Times
text:
Model design

Use star schema, not flat tables
A fact table surrounded by dimension tables lets the VertiPaq engine compress data far more efficiently than a single wide flat table. If your model is one giant table with 80 columns, restructure it.


Remove unused columns
Every column you import lives in memory. If you’re not using CustomerMiddleName in any visual or DAX, drop it in Power Query before loading. This alone can halve model size on data-rich tables.


Choose the right storage mode



Scenario
Storage Mode




Data fits in memory, fast queries needed
Import


Huge data, acceptable query latency
DirectQuery


Mix of large + small tables
Composite


Fabric Lakehouse, near-real-time
Direct Lake



DirectQuery reports are almost always slower than Import — only use it when data volumes or freshness requirements force your hand.

title: Optimizing Power BI Report Load Times
text:
Visuals and report design

Reduce visuals per page. Each visual triggers at least one DAX query. A report with 25 visuals on one page fires 25+ queries on load.
Use Bookmarks to show/hide visuals rather than putting everything on the canvas at once.
Avoid high-cardinality slicers. A slicer on a column with 50,000 unique values is expensive to render.
Disable “show items with no data” on visuals unless you need it — it forces additional queries.

title: Optimizing Power BI Report Load Times
text:
The biggest win I’ve seen
The single biggest improvement I’ve ever gotten: removing unused relationships from the data model. Invisible bidirectional relationships that “seemed harmless” were forcing the engine to walk the full graph on every query. Removing two unused many-to-many relationships cut a dashboard from 22 seconds to 4 seconds.
Always audit your relationships after a model has been in production for a while. Relationships accumulate. Not all of them should stay.

title: Why I’m Betting on Microsoft Fabric for 2026
text: A year ago I wrote about migrating to Microsoft Fabric. A year in production later — here’s the updated take.

title: Why I’m Betting on Microsoft Fabric for 2026
text:
What I got wrong
My initial concern was that Fabric was “too new” for serious production use. That was wrong. The platform has matured quickly, Microsoft’s investment has been relentless, and the community has grown substantially.
I also underestimated OneLake. Having a single logical data lake that all Fabric workloads read from — no copying, no ETL between tools — is genuinely transformative for how you architect data platforms.

title: Why I’m Betting on Microsoft Fabric for 2026
text:
What Fabric does well now
Direct Lake is production-ready. This was experimental a year ago. Today it’s the default recommendation for large Power BI semantic models, and it delivers on the promise: fast queries against Delta tables without import refresh cycles.
Fabric notebooks + lakehouses are a solid alternative to Databricks for organizations already in the Microsoft ecosystem. Not as mature, but the integration story is significantly simpler.
Data pipelines have improved. The Gen2 Dataflow and Data Factory pipeline experience inside Fabric is now genuinely better than standalone ADF for most use cases I encounter.

title: Why I’m Betting on Microsoft Fabric for 2026
text:
What still needs work
Monitoring and observability is still patchy. Understanding exactly what’s running, what’s consuming capacity, and what’s slow requires jumping between multiple tools. A unified observability layer would be a huge quality-of-life improvement.
Capacity management can catch you off-guard. Fabric uses a consumption-based model, and a runaway query or a poorly optimized notebook can spike capacity unexpectedly. Budget alerts and capacity smoothing help, but it requires active management.

title: Why I’m Betting on Microsoft Fabric for 2026
text:
The platform bet
Here’s why I’m all-in: Microsoft has committed to making Fabric the unified data platform across the entire Azure data stack. Every piece — Synapse, ADF, Power BI, Purview, Machine Learning — is converging into Fabric. For organizations on Azure, the long-term trajectory is clear.
For consultants and developers who build on the Microsoft stack, Fabric fluency is quickly becoming table stakes. Not “nice to have” — required.
If you haven’t gotten hands-on with it yet, the Microsoft Fabric free trial gives you a Fabric capacity to experiment with. Start there.

title: 5 DAX Patterns That Changed How I Build Reports
text: After years of writing DAX professionally, certain patterns have become second nature. These five aren’t in most beginner tutorials, but they come up constantly in real work.

title: 5 DAX Patterns That Changed How I Build Reports
text:
1. The SELECTEDVALUE safety pattern
SELECTEDVALUE returns the value of a column when the filter context contains exactly one distinct value — otherwise returns the alternate result. I use it constantly for dynamic titles and conditional measures.
SelectedRegion =
SELECTEDVALUE(Regions[RegionName], "All Regions")
This is safer than VALUES(...)[0] or FIRSTNONBLANK, which can error or behave unexpectedly with multi-select slicers.

title: 5 DAX Patterns That Changed How I Build Reports
text:
2. Rolling N-period calculation
The classic rolling average pattern, but generalized for any period selection:
Rolling3MonthSales =
VAR MonthsToInclude = 3
VAR LastDate = MAX(Calendar[Date])
VAR FirstDate = EDATE(LastDate, -MonthsToInclude + 1)
RETURN
    CALCULATE(
        [TotalSales],
        Calendar[Date] >= EOMONTH(FirstDate, -1) + 1,
        Calendar[Date] <= LastDate,
        ALL(Calendar)
    )
Parameterizing MonthsToInclude and driving it from a slicer gives you a self-service rolling window without multiple hardcoded measures.

title: 5 DAX Patterns That Changed How I Build Reports
text:
3. Disconnected table for what-if parameters
A disconnected table (no relationships to anything) combined with SELECTEDVALUE is the cleanest way to build what-if scenario inputs:
-- Disconnected table: DiscountRates with values 0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20
SelectedDiscount =
SELECTEDVALUE(DiscountRates[Rate], 0)

ProjectedRevenue =
[BaseRevenue] * (1 - [SelectedDiscount])
Users pick a discount rate from a slicer; the measure responds. No parameters, no what-if wizard quirks.

title: 5 DAX Patterns That Changed How I Build Reports
text:
4. KEEPFILTERS for additive filtering
KEEPFILTERS is CALCULATE’s underappreciated sibling. Without it, CALCULATE replaces the existing filter context. With it, it intersects:
-- Without KEEPFILTERS: shows West Coast sales regardless of what's selected
WestCoastSales =
CALCULATE([TotalSales], Region[Area] = "West Coast")

-- With KEEPFILTERS: only shows West Coast IF West Coast is in the current filter context
WestCoastSales_Restricted =
CALCULATE([TotalSales], KEEPFILTERS(Region[Area] = "West Coast"))
I use KEEPFILTERS whenever I want a measure to “respect” slicer selections while also adding its own filter.

title: 5 DAX Patterns That Changed How I Build Reports
text:
5. The virtual relationship pattern
Sometimes you need a filter to flow across tables that don’t have a physical relationship — or the physical relationship points the wrong way. TREATAS creates a virtual relationship at query time:
BudgetForSelectedProducts =
CALCULATE(
    SUM(Budget[Amount]),
    TREATAS(VALUES(Products[ProductID]), Budget[ProductID])
)
This takes the current filter context on Products[ProductID] and applies it as if it were a filter on Budget[ProductID], without a physical relationship. Extremely useful in composite models or when bridging fact tables.

DAX rewards pattern recognition. Once you’ve solved a problem the right way once, you’ll recognize the same shape in different contexts. These five patterns cover an enormous percentage of real reporting challenges.
What patterns do you find yourself reaching for? I’d love to hear — find me on LinkedIn.

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text: Most analytics pipelines are batch-based: pull data nightly, transform, load, refresh. That works until your stakeholders ask “but what’s happening right now?” Here’s how we solved it at Umojo.

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
The use case
We track curb occupancy in real time — sensors send events every few seconds indicating whether a curb space is occupied, blocked, or available. City operations teams need live dashboards, not hourly snapshots.
Requirements: - Sub-minute dashboard latency - Handle ~5,000 events/second at peak - Aggregate occupancy % by zone, by block, by city - Power BI as the visualization layer

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
Why Azure Stream Analytics
We evaluated Kafka + custom consumers and Azure Event Hubs + Functions before landing on Stream Analytics (ASA). The key selling points:

SQL-like query language — our team writes a lot of T-SQL; the ASA query model was immediately familiar
Native Power BI output — ASA can push directly to a Power BI streaming dataset without any glue code
Windowing functions built-in — tumbling, hopping, and sliding windows are first-class
Managed service — no cluster to maintain

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
The pipeline
IoT Sensors
    │
    ▼
Azure Event Hub  (ingestion)
    │
    ▼
Azure Stream Analytics  (transformation)
    │
    ├──► Power BI Streaming Dataset  (live dashboard)
    │
    └──► Azure Data Lake / Delta  (historical archive)

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
The ASA query
The core transformation aggregates sensor events into 1-minute tumbling windows:
SELECT
    Zone,
    Block,
    CityID,
    COUNT(*) AS TotalSpaces,
    SUM(CASE WHEN Status = 'Occupied' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS OccupiedSpaces,
    CAST(
        SUM(CASE WHEN Status = 'Occupied' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS FLOAT
    ) / COUNT(*) * 100 AS OccupancyPct,
    System.Timestamp() AS WindowEnd
INTO [PowerBIOutput]
FROM [EventHubInput] TIMESTAMP BY EventTime
GROUP BY
    Zone, Block, CityID,
    TumblingWindow(minute, 1)
One window, four output metrics, feeding live into Power BI.

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
Power BI streaming dataset
The ASA output targets a Power BI streaming dataset — a special dataset type that accepts REST push and doesn’t persist historical data by default (you can enable historic analysis to keep a rolling window).
In the Power BI report, tiles connected to streaming datasets update automatically without manual refresh. The dashboard latency we achieved: 18–35 seconds end-to-end from sensor event to dashboard update.

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
Handling late-arriving events
Sensors occasionally batch-send events due to network interruptions. ASA has native late-arrival tolerance:
Late arrival tolerance: 5 minutes
Out-of-order tolerance: 30 seconds
This prevents dropped events while keeping the window calculation accurate.

title: Real-Time Analytics with Azure Stream Analytics
text:
Cost considerations
ASA is billed per Streaming Unit (SU). We run 3 SUs for our peak load, which handles ~5,000 events/second comfortably. Right-size before going to production — over-provisioning SUs is the most common cost mistake I’ve seen.

The full stack — Event Hub → Stream Analytics → Power BI — took about two weeks to build and tune. The operational simplicity has been worth it. If you’re building something similar, I’m happy to chat.

title: Migrating from Power BI Premium to Microsoft Fabric
text: At Umojo, I led the migration of our entire Power BI Premium infrastructure to Microsoft Fabric over Q4 2024 into early 2025. Here’s what I learned.

title: Migrating from Power BI Premium to Microsoft Fabric
text:
Why migrate?
Power BI Premium per-capacity was getting expensive relative to what we were actually using. Microsoft Fabric promised a more unified, consumption-based model and, more importantly, a path toward lakehouse architecture that would simplify our entire data stack.
The pitch: one platform for data engineering, warehousing, BI, and ML. We were already deep in the Microsoft ecosystem, so this was a natural evolution.

title: Migrating from Power BI Premium to Microsoft Fabric
text:
What the migration actually involved

1. Capacity and workspace setup
The first thing to understand is that Fabric workspaces are not the same as Power BI workspaces, even though they look similar. You’ll need to create a new Fabric-enabled workspace and migrate items into it.
Old: Power BI Premium Workspace
     └── Datasets (PBIX)
     └── Reports
     └── Dataflows (Gen1)

New: Fabric Workspace
     └── Lakehouses
     └── Semantic Models (upgraded datasets)
     └── Reports
     └── Dataflows Gen2


2. Datasets → Semantic Models
Microsoft rebranded datasets to “Semantic Models” in Fabric, but the bigger change is how they’re managed. Direct Lake mode became available, allowing semantic models to read directly from Delta tables in a Lakehouse with near-zero import latency.
We had 23 datasets. About 18 migrated cleanly. The remaining 5 had complex composite models or custom connectors that needed manual rework.


3. Dataflows Gen1 → Gen2
This was the most painful part. Gen1 Dataflows don’t automatically upgrade — you have to rebuild them in Gen2 from scratch, or export and re-import the M query code.
Tip: Export your Gen1 queries to .json and use them as a reference while rebuilding in Gen2. Don’t try to copy-paste M code blindly — some functions behave differently.


4. Gateway migration
On-premises data gateways largely carried over, but we had to re-register connections in the new Fabric workspace. Budget time for this — especially if you have many datasources or shared gateway clusters.

title: Migrating from Power BI Premium to Microsoft Fabric
text:
Performance observations
After migration, report load times improved noticeably for our Direct Lake models — the elimination of import refresh cycles alone was worth it. However, some models that previously cached well saw slightly higher query times initially, as Direct Lake queries hit the lakehouse storage more frequently.
The fix was proper Delta table optimization: running OPTIMIZE and VACUUM on hot tables brought query performance back in line.

title: Migrating from Power BI Premium to Microsoft Fabric
text:
What I’d do differently

Don’t migrate everything at once. We tried to do too much in one sprint. A phased migration by priority gave us much better outcomes in the second half.
Test Direct Lake thoroughly before committing. Some of our DAX measures behaved unexpectedly in Direct Lake mode vs. Import mode. Document your known patterns before switching.
Communicate early with stakeholders. Report refresh schedules changed during migration. A few teams were surprised. Avoid this.

title: Migrating from Power BI Premium to Microsoft Fabric
text:
Final thoughts
Overall, the migration was worth it. We’re on a more modern platform, costs are more predictable, and having data engineering and BI in one place has already simplified several workflows. But go in with realistic expectations — it’s not a flip-of-a-switch.
If you’re considering a similar migration and have questions, reach out.

title: Power Apps Canvas vs. Model-Driven: When to Use Which
text: Every time I start a new Power Apps project, someone asks: “Canvas or Model-Driven?” The answer is almost never obvious from the requirement document alone. Here’s the framework I use.

title: Power Apps Canvas vs. Model-Driven: When to Use Which
text:
The core difference
Canvas apps give you pixel-perfect control over the UI. You design screens from scratch, place components where you want, and connect to virtually any datasource (SharePoint, SQL, Dataverse, REST APIs, Excel…). Think of it like building a webpage.
Model-Driven apps are generated from your data model in Dataverse. You define entities, relationships, and forms — the UI is mostly auto-generated. Less visual flexibility, but more structured and faster to build for standard CRUD workflows.

title: Power Apps Canvas vs. Model-Driven: When to Use Which
text:
When Canvas wins
Use Canvas when:

The UX is non-standard. Custom layouts, branded interfaces, unusual navigation — Canvas is the only option.
Your datasource isn’t Dataverse. SharePoint lists, SQL Server, REST APIs — Canvas handles them natively. Model-Driven requires Dataverse.
It’s a task-specific tool. A mobile form for field inspections, a kiosk interface, a focused data entry screen — Canvas excels here.
The user base is small. Canvas apps require per-user licensing; for small teams this is manageable.

Example: At Raimbek Group, I built a Canvas app for field sales reps to enter daily visit logs on mobile. Custom map integration, offline-capable, branded UI. Model-Driven would have been the wrong choice.

title: Power Apps Canvas vs. Model-Driven: When to Use Which
text:
When Model-Driven wins
Use Model-Driven when:

You have complex relational data. Entities with many relationships, lookups, and business rules — Dataverse and Model-Driven handle this elegantly.
You need built-in features. Business Process Flows, role-based security, audit trails, and dashboards come for free. Building these in Canvas is expensive.
Power Automate workflows are central. Dataverse + Power Automate integration is seamless. Trigger flows on record creation/update without custom connectors.
Multiple apps share the same data. Dataverse is a shared layer. Multiple Model-Driven (and Canvas) apps can work on the same data without duplication.

Example: At Chevron (TCO), we built a Model-Driven app for QA/QC project tracking — multiple entities (Projects, Inspections, Non-Conformances), complex relationships, approval workflows via Power Automate. Canvas would have taken 3x longer.

title: Power Apps Canvas vs. Model-Driven: When to Use Which
text:
The hybrid approach
For projects where neither is a clean fit, combine both: use Model-Driven as the backbone (Dataverse, security, workflows) and embed Canvas apps as custom pages inside the Model-Driven experience.
This is the pattern Microsoft is pushing with Custom Pages — Canvas-designed screens that live within a Model-Driven app. It’s the best of both worlds, at the cost of additional complexity.

title: Power Apps Canvas vs. Model-Driven: When to Use Which
text:
Quick decision table



Requirement
Canvas
Model-Driven




Custom branding/layout
✅
❌


Non-Dataverse data
✅
❌


Complex entity relationships
❌
✅


Business Process Flows
❌
✅


Audit trails (built-in)
❌
✅


Mobile-optimized
✅
Limited


Fast CRUD UI
❌
✅



When in doubt, ask: who will maintain this in 2 years? Model-Driven apps are easier to maintain as organizational data models evolve. Canvas apps tend to accumulate technical debt faster when requirements change.

title: Row-Level Security in Power BI: A Practical Guide
text: Row-Level Security (RLS) is one of those features that looks simple until you’re implementing it for a real production deployment. Here’s a practical guide based on what I’ve built at Umojo.

title: Row-Level Security in Power BI: A Practical Guide
section: The two flavors: Static vs. Dynamic
text:
The two flavors: Static vs. Dynamic
Static RLS means you hardcode filter values into a role. Useful for simple, well-defined groups:
[Region] = "North America"
This works great when your user groups are stable and small. When you have 200 tenant-specific views, it breaks down immediately.
Dynamic RLS uses DAX functions to make filtering data-driven, keying off the currently authenticated user:
[Email] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()
This is what most production systems need. One role, infinite tenants.

title: Row-Level Security in Power BI: A Practical Guide
text:
The security table pattern
The pattern I use most often: a dedicated security table that maps users to their accessible data.
SecurityTable
├── UserEmail (text)
├── RegionID (int)
└── ClientID (int)
Your RLS DAX then becomes:
[ClientID] IN
  CALCULATETABLE(
    VALUES(SecurityTable[ClientID]),
    SecurityTable[UserEmail] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()
  )
This approach decouples access control from your data model, making it easy to add/remove access without touching DAX or Power BI Desktop.

title: Row-Level Security in Power BI: A Practical Guide
Row-Level Security in Power BI: A Practical Guide
text:
Common pitfalls
1. Testing with your own account: You’ll always see everything unless you explicitly test with “View as role” in Power BI Desktop. Build a test matrix and use it religiously.
2. Forgetting bidirectional cross-filter: If your security table has a relationship to a bridge table, make sure cross-filter direction is set correctly, or users will see data they shouldn’t.
3. USERPRINCIPALNAME vs. USERNAME: In cloud, UPN is the email. In on-premises gateway scenarios, USERNAME returns the Windows identity. Know your environment.
4. Service Principal embedding: When using service principals for embedding, the identities array must include a dataset reference or the token generation will fail silently.

title: Row-Level Security in Power BI: A Practical Guide
text:
Validating RLS in production
Don’t trust your staging environment alone. I always build a small validation report that shows each user exactly which rows they’re seeing, and cross-reference against the security table. Surfacing this to the right ops person early catches misconfigured access before clients notice.

Questions? Find me on LinkedIn or drop me a line.
